title: Over forty years of research towards the understanding of Quantum Brownian Motion -- the contributions of A. O. Caldeira
authors: Marcus V. S. Bonança, Sebastian Deffner, Gert-Ludwig Ingold
abstract: This article presents a brief account of Amir O. Caldeira's contributions to the theory of quantum Brownian motion. Motivated by its importance, we outline the description of Brownian motion in the quantum regime following Caldeira's first works. In this context, we particularly highlight the effect of dissipation on the tunneling rate out of a metastable state. We then journey along the alternative ways to approach quantum Brownian motion developed by Caldeira during his career, which go beyond the so-called Caldeira-Leggett model. We conclude by summarizing some of Caldeira's contributions to contemporary fields such as the theory of quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics, that were strongly inspired by his eponymous approach to quantum Brownian motion.
summary: This review article chronicles the scientific contributions of A. O. Caldeira to the field of Quantum Brownian Motion. It details the transition from classical to quantum dissipative dynamics, highlighting the importance of the Caldeira-Leggett model in understanding tunneling and decoherence. The authors also explore advanced models involving scattering and the implications of these theories for modern quantum thermodynamics and the NISQ era. Ultimately, it connects foundational work in dissipation to contemporary problems in quantum information and many-body dynamics.
main_problem: The paper reviews the forty-year evolution of the theory of Quantum Brownian Motion (QBM), focusing on how dissipation and noise influence quantum dynamics, tunneling, and decoherence.
main_result: It traces the development from the Caldeira-Leggett model to advanced scattering models and demonstrates how these theories underpin modern quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics.
method: The review synthesizes various theoretical frameworks, including path-integral formulations (Feynman-Vernon influence functional), Langevin equations, and quantum master equations.
model_or_system: The primary models discussed are the Caldeira-Leggett model (system coupled to a harmonic oscillator reservoir) and scattering models (momentum-based bilinear coupling), applied to systems like Josephson junctions, LCR circuits, and solitons.
key_observables: Tunneling rates, diffusion coefficients, quantum coherences, entropy production, and heat current.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), friction/damping coefficient (eta), coupling strength, and frequency scales (thermal vs. friction scales).
main_assumptions: Assumptions include the use of minimal system-plus-environment models and, in some cases, the neglect of initial system-environment correlations (factorizing initial conditions).
paper_structure: The paper follows a historical and thematic progression: starting with the foundational Caldeira-Leggett model and its impact on tunneling, moving to alternative scattering-based approaches, and concluding with applications in decoherence theory and quantum thermodynamics.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive historical and theoretical roadmap for anyone studying open quantum systems, specifically regarding the transition from quantum to classical regimes and the challenges of defining thermodynamics in the strong-coupling regime.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
